source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 10.1.5-10.1.8 tools and conventions (pp. 174-177)

questions.q: Which of these are command-line backup tools mentioned by the guide?
options: tar, cpio, pax, dd, dump
zip, rar, 7z, arj
cp, mv, rm, ln
grep, sed, awk, cut
questions.why: The command-line backup tools listed are tar, cpio, pax, dd and dump; this chapter focuses on tar and cpio.

questions.q: Which statement about tar versus cpio is correct?
options: tar is easy to use and can add files to an existing backup; cpio retains owners/groups/dates/rights and skips damaged files
cpio cannot preserve ownership; tar can never add files
tar skips damaged files; cpio cannot back up a whole file system
They are identical in every respect
questions.why: tar is simple and supports appending; cpio preserves owners/groups/dates/rights, skips damaged files, and can handle the whole file system.

questions.q: Why use a naming convention (directory, utility, options, date) for backup files?
options: To quickly identify a backup's contents and avoid a hazardous restoration
Because Linux requires file extensions
To compress the backup automatically
To make the backup smaller
questions.why: An explicit name (e.g. home.A.tar.gz with a date) tells you the contents and format at a glance, reducing restore mistakes.

questions.q: What do the two storage methods, internal and external, mean?
options: Internal stores backups on the current working disk; external stores them on separate devices (USB, disk, server, NAS)
Internal means compressed; external means uncompressed
Internal means full backup; external means incremental
Internal is on tape; external is on disk
questions.why: Internal keeps backups on the working disk (risky); external puts them on separate media such as USB drives, servers or NAS.

questions.q: If you see a file with a .tar.gz or .tgz extension, what does that tell you?
options: It is a tar archive compressed with gzip - the suffix aids human recognition even though Linux does not require extensions
The extension is mandatory for the file to work
It is an executable program
It is a plain text file
questions.why: Most Linux files need no extension; suffixes like .tar.gz/.tgz are added for humans to recognise the format and handle it correctly.
